revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:別所茶臼山古墳]][[zh:別所茶臼山古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|36.297089|N|139.333414|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}}}
'''Bessho Chausuyama Kofun''' (別所茶臼山古墳, also known as '''Enpukuji Chausuyama Kofun''' or '''Hōsen Chausuyama Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Ōta, Gunma|en|Ōta, Gunma|qid=Q386179|lt=Ōta, Gunma|1=Ōta, Gunma}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. This keyhole-shaped Kofun ({{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}) is designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] as part of the {{ill|Nitta Manor Ruins|ja|新田荘遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nitta Manor Ruins|qq=}}. == Overview == The Bessho Chausuyama Kofun is situated in the central part of Ōta City on the western edge of the Hōsen Plateau (Yura Plateau). It is now located within the grounds of {{ill|Enpukuji Temple (Ōta)|ja|円福寺 (太田市別所町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enpukuji Temple (Ōta)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the Twelve Shrine ({{ill|Jūnisho Shrine|ja|十二所神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūnisho Shrine|qq=}}), with the Hōsen Kita Kindergarten adjacent to it. The mound has a keyhole shape, oriented with the front section facing south. The rear circular portion is constructed in three tiers, while the front rectangular portion has two tiers. The mound measures 164.5 meters (or 168 meters, depending on the source) in length, making it the third-largest kofun in Gunma Prefecture, following the {{ill|Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|en|Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|qid=Q11444167|lt=Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|1=Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun}} (210 meters) and {{ill|Sengenyama Kofun|ja|浅間山古墳 (高崎市倉賀野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengenyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浅間山古墳 (高崎市)}} (171.5 meters). The mound's surface is covered with river stones ({{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|qid=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}}), and cylindrical haniwa (clay figures) were placed around its middle tier. Currently, parts of the mound have been excavated to construct temple buildings, including the main hall of Enpukuji on its lower part, the Twelve Shrine on the rear circular portion, and smaller halls in the central and front sections. A horseshoe-shaped moat originally surrounded the mound, but most of it has been buried, with remnants visible only on the north side of the rear circular portion. The Bessho Chausuyama Kofun is estimated to have been constructed in the early 5th century during the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. It is one of the representative Kofuns of eastern Gunma and a contemporary of the Sengenyama Kofun in western Gunma. It is believed that the successors of the person interred in this kofun eventually unified the Gunma region, culminating in the construction of the massive Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun, the largest in eastern Japan. The site was designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|Historic Sites of Japan]] in 2000 as part of the "Nitta Manor Ruins." == Archaeological History == * '''1938''': Included in the "Jōmō Kofun Sōran" as "Hōsen Village Kofun No. 5." * '''1971''': Designated as a Gunma Prefectural Historic Site under the name "Enpukuji Chausuyama Kofun and the Tombs of the Nitta Clan, Including Stone Lanterns." * '''1991-1992''': Excavation and investigation of the eastern side of the moat. * '''2000''': Designated as a National Historic Site (included in the "Nitta Manor Ruins"). Prefectural designation was rescinded. == Mound Dimensions == The mound's dimensions are as follows: * Total length: 164.5 meters (other sources state 168 meters) * Rear circular portion: ** Diameter: 96 meters ** Height: 11.8 meters (14 meters according to other sources) * Front rectangular portion: ** Height: 7.3 meters (9 meters according to other sources) == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * Site Information Board (Ōta City Board of Education, 2002) * {{Cite book|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17213509}} 10 Gunma Prefecture|editor=|author=|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490107|chapter=Enpukuji Chausuyama Kofun|year=1987|qq=}} * {{Cite book|title=Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|editor=|author=Shimada Takao|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|isbn=978-4490105995|chapter=Bessho Chausuyama Kofun|year=2002|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|en|Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|qid=Q11444167|lt=Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun|1=Ōta Tenjinyama Kofun}} == External Links == * [http://www.gunmaibun.org/remain/guide/tomo/tyausu.html Bessho Chausuyama Kofun] - Gunma Prefectural Archaeological Research Institute * [http://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunmazai/otabunka45.html Enpukuji Chausuyama Kofun] - Ōta City Website
{{translated page|ja|別所茶臼山古墳|version=101357185|comment=Imported full ja history}}
